elements.sent-2411-001: Federated Learning (FL) enables multiple clients, such as mobile phones and IoT devices, to collaboratively train a global machine learning model while keeping their data localized.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, recent studies have revealed that the training phase of FL is vulnerable to reconstruction attacks, such as attribute inference attacks (AIA), where adversaries exploit exchanged messages and auxiliary public information to uncover sensitive attributes of targeted clients.
elements.sent-2411-003: While these attacks have been extensively studied in the context of classification tasks, their impact on regression tasks remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we address this gap by proposing novel model-based AIAs specifically designed for regression tasks in FL environments.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach considers scenarios where adversaries can either eavesdrop on exchanged messages or directly interfere with the training process.
elements.sent-2411-006: We benchmark our proposed attacks against state-of-the-art methods using real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results demonstrate a significant increase in reconstruction accuracy, particularly in heterogeneous client datasets, a common scenario in FL.
elements.sent-2411-008: The efficacy of our model-based AIAs makes them better candidates for empirically quantifying privacy leakage for federated regression tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Federated learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train a global model~\cite{mcmahan2017communication, lian17, li2018federated .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Since clients' data is not collected by a third party, FL naturally offers a certain level of privacy.
elements.sent-2411-011: Nevertheless, FL alone does not provide formal privacy guarantees, and recent works have demonstrated that clients' private information can still be easily leaked~\cite{surveyAttacks, liu2022threats .}
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, an adversary with access to the exchanged messages and knowledge of some public information (e.g., client's provided ratings)~\cite{attribute_fl_workshop, attribute_fl, feng2021attribute can reconstruct a client's sensitive attributes (e.g., gender/religion) in an attack known as attribute inference attack (AIA).}
elements.sent-2411-013: Additionally, the adversary can reconstruct client's training samples such as images~\cite{geiping2020inverting, yin2021see .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{figures/income/dataset_size_effect.png} \caption{Average performance of different AIAs when four clients train a neural network through FedAvg with 1 local epoch and batch size 32. Each client stores $|\localdataset|$ data points randomly sampled from ACS Income dataset~\cite{income_dataset}. The adversary infers the gender attribute of every data sample held by the client given access to the released (public) information.} \label{fig:gradient_vs_model}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, these reconstruction attacks for FL have primarily been tested on classification tasks and have not been explored for regression tasks, which are, needless to say, equally important for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-015: Quite surprisingly, our experiments, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:gradient_vs_model , demonstrate that the accuracy of state-of-the-art gradient-based AIA under a honest-but-curious adversary~\cite{attribute_fl_workshop, attribute_fl (referred to as passive) drops significantly from $71\%$ on a classification task to $50\%$ (random guess) on a regression task once the targeted client holds more than 256 data points.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, even a more powerful (active) adversary capable of forging the messages to the targeted client to extract more information~\cite{attribute_fl_workshop, attribute_fl offers only limited improvement to the AIA performance on regression tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Detailed information about this experiment is in Appendix~\ref{app:figure1 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we show that federated training of regression tasks does not inherently enjoy higher privacy, but it is simply more vulnerable to other forms of attacks.
elements.sent-2411-019: While existing FL AIA attacks for classification tasks are gradient-based (see Sec.~\ref{subsec:gradient-based ), we show that model-based AIAs---initially proposed for centralized training~\cite{fredrikson2014privacy, kasiviswanathan2013power, yeom2018privacy ---may be more effective for regression tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Figure~\ref{fig:gradient_vs_model illustrates that a model-based attack on the server's global model (i.e., the final model trained through FL) already performs at least as well as the SOTA gradient-based passive attack.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, it highlights that even more powerful attacks (up to 30~p.p.~more accurate) could be launched if the adversary had access to the optimal local model of the targeted client (i.e., a model trained only on the client's dataset).
elements.sent-2411-022: Motivated by these observations, we propose a new two-step model-based AIA for federated regression tasks.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this attack, the adversary first (approximately) reconstructs the client's optimal local model and then applies an existing model-based AIA to that model.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-025: We provide an analytical lower bound for model-based AIA accuracy in the least squares regression problem.
elements.sent-2411-026: This result motivates the adversary's strategy to approximate the client's optimal local model in federated regression tasks.. (Sec.~\ref{sec:model-based-guarantees ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose methods for approximating optimal local models where adversaries can either eavesdrop on exchanged messages or directly interfere with the training process (Sec.~\ref{sec:lmra ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our experiments show that our model-based AIAs are better candidates for empirically quantifying privacy leakage for federated regression tasks (Sec.~\ref{sec:exp ).}

elements.sent-2411-032: In FL, clients cooperate to learn a global model, which minimizes the following empirical risk over all the data owned by clients: \begin{equation}\label{eq:globalobjective} \min_{\model \in \real^d} \globallossfunction(\model) = \sum_{c \in \clients} p_c \locallossfunction(\model, \dataset_c) \notag = \sum_{c \in \clients} p_c \left( \frac{1}{S_c} \sum_{i=1}^{S_c} \ell(\model, \x(i), y_c(i)) \right).\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{ FL Framework}\label{algo:fl} Output: $\model^T$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] { \item[Server $s$:] \FOR{$t\in \{0,...,T-1\}$} \STATE{$s$ selects a subset of the clients $\subclients^t\subseteq \clients$, }\label{algoline:select_client} \STATE{$s$ broadcasts the global model $\model^t$ to $\subclients^t$,} \label{algoline:broadcast} \STATE{$s$ waits for the updated models $\model_c^t$ from every client $c\in \subclients^t$,} \STATE{$s$ computes $\model^{t+1}$ by aggregating the received updated models.} \label{algoline:aggregation} \ENDFOR \item[Client $c\in \clients$: Input $\model$, Output $\model_c$] \WHILE{FL training is not completed} \STATE{$c$ listens for the arrival of new global model $\model$,} \STATE{$c$ updates its local model: $\model_c \leftarrow \localupdaterule^c (\model, \dataset_c)$\label{algoline:updaterule}} \STATE{$c$ sends back $\model_c$ to the server.} \ENDWHILE } \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Client $c$'s local update rule ($\localupdaterule^c(\model^t, \localdataset)$) in FedAvg~\cite{mcmahan2017communication}}\label{algo:fedavg} Input: server model $\model^t$, local dataset $\localdataset$, batch size $B$, number of local epochs $E$, learning rate~$\eta$. \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE{$\model_c^t(0) \leftarrow \model^t$, $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow$ (split $\localdataset$ into batches of size $B$), $k\leftarrow 0$} \FOR{each local epoch $e$ from 1 to $E$} \FOR{batch $b \in \mathcal{B}$} \STATE{$\model_c^t(k+1) \leftarrow \model_c^t(k) - \eta \times \gradient (\model_c^t(k),b)$, \,\,\, where $\gradient(\model_c^t(k), b) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{x\in b} \nabla \ell(\model_c^t(k), x)$} \label{algoline:gradient_update} \STATE{$k\leftarrow k+1$} \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE{Return $\model_c^t(K)$, where $K=E\lceil\frac{S_c}{B}\rceil$} \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-004: Reconstructing the local model in FL
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section, we show how an adversary may reconstruct the optimal local model of client $c$, i.e.,~$\localmodel = \arg\min_{\model\in \real^d \locallossfunction(\model, \localdataset)$.}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Reconstruction of client-$c$ local model by an active adversary $a$}\label{algo:active} Input: Let $\mathcal{T}^a_c $ be set of rounds during which the adversary attacks client $c$ and $\model^a_c$ be the corresponding malicious model. \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE{$\model_c^a \leftarrow$ latest model received from client $c$} \FOR{$t\in \mathcal{T}^a_c$} \STATE{$a$ sends the model $\model^a_c$ to client $c$,} \STATE{$a$ waits the updated model from $\model_c$ from client $c$,} \STATE{$a$ computes the pseudo-gradient $\model^a_c-\model_c$ and updates $\model^a_c$ and the corresponding moment vectors following Adam~\cite[Alg.~1]{adam},} \ENDFOR \STATE{Return $\model^a_c$ as the estimator for client $c$'s local model} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-123: This dataset includes 1,339 records and 6 features: age, gender, BMI, number of children, smoking status, and region.
elements.sent-2411-124: The regression task is to predict each individual's medical charges billed by health insurance.
elements.sent-2411-125: The dataset is split i.i.d.. between 2 clients.
elements.sent-2411-126: This dataset contains census information from 50 U.S.
elements.sent-2411-127: It includes 15 features related to demographic information such as age, occupation, and education level.
elements.sent-2411-128: The regression task is to predict an individual's income.
elements.sent-2411-129: We investigate two FL scenarios, named Income-L and Income-A, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-130: In Income-L, there are 10 clients holding only records from the state of Louisiana (15,982 records in total).
elements.sent-2411-131: These clients can be viewed as the local entities working for the Louisiana State Census Data Center.
elements.sent-2411-132: We examine various levels of statistical heterogeneity among these local entities, with the splitting strategy detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:datasplit_incomel .}
elements.sent-2411-133: In Income-A, there are 51 clients, each representing a census region and collectively covering all regions.
elements.sent-2411-134: Every client randomly selects 20\% of the data points from the corresponding census region, resulting in a total of 332,900 records.
elements.sent-2411-135: For all the datasets, each client keeps $90\%$ of its data for training and uses $10\%$ for validation.
elements.sent-2411-136: In all the experiments, each client follows FedAvg (Alg.~\ref{algo:fedavg ) to train a neural network model with a single hidden layer of 128 neurons, using ReLU as activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The number of communication rounds is fixed to $T = \lceil 100/E \rceil$ where $E$ is the number of local epochs.
elements.sent-2411-138: Each client participates to all rounds, i.e.,~$\mathcal{T _c = \{0,...,T-1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The learning rate is tuned for each training scenario (different datasets and number of local epochs), with values provided in Appendix~\ref{app:hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-140: The passive adversary may eavesdrop all the exchanged messages until the end of the training.
elements.sent-2411-141: The active adversary launches the attack after $T$ rounds\footnote{We also examine the impact of the attack's starting round.}
elements.sent-2411-142: The results are presented in the Appendix~\ref{app:starting_points .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Every attack is evaluated over FL trainings from 3 different random seeds.
elements.sent-2411-144: For Medical dataset, the adversary infers whether a person smokes or not.
elements.sent-2411-145: For Income-L and Income-A datasets, the adversary infers the gender.
elements.sent-2411-146: The AIA accuracy is the fraction of the correct inferences over all the samples.
elements.sent-2411-147: We have also conducted experiments for federated least squares regression on the same datasets.
elements.sent-2411-148: The results can be found in Appendix~\ref{app:linear_model .}
elements.sent-2411-149: We compare our method with the (gradient-based) SOTA (Sec.~\ref{subsec:gradient-based ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: The baseline performance is affected by the set of inspected rounds~$\mathcal{T}$ considered in~\eqref{eq:aia_gradient_based .}
elements.sent-2411-151: We select the inspected rounds $\mathcal{T}$ based on two criteria: the highest cosine similarity and the best AIA accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-152: In a real attack, the adversary is not expected to know the attack accuracy beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-153: Therefore, we refer to the attack based on the highest cosine similarity as {\bf{Grad and to the other as {\bf{Grad-w-O ({\bf Grad ient {\bf w ith {\bf O racle), as it assumes the existence of an oracle that provides the attack accuracy.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The details for the tuning of $\mathcal{T}$ and other hyper-parameter settings can be found in Appendix~~\ref{app:hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-155: Our attacks consist of two steps: 1) reconstructing the optimal local model, and 2) executing the model-based AIA~\eqref{eq:aia_model_based .}
elements.sent-2411-156: For the first step, a passive adversary uses the last-returned model from the targeted client, while an active adversary executes Alg.~\ref{algo:active .}
elements.sent-2411-157: The details of the hyperparameter settings can be found in Appendix~\ref{app:hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-158: To provide an upper bound on the performance of our approach, we assume the existence of an oracle that provides the optimal local model for the first step of our attack.
elements.sent-2411-159: In practice, the optimal local model is determined empirically by running Adam for a very large number of iterations.

elements.sent-2411-160: From Table~\ref{tab:attacks_32 , we see that our attacks outperform gradient-based ones in both passive and active scenarios across all three datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Notably, our passive attack achieves improvements of over $15$ and $8$ percentage points (p.p.) for the Income-L and Medical datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-162: Even when the gradient-based method has access to an oracle, our passive attacks still achieves higher accuracy on two datasets and comes very close on Income-A.
elements.sent-2411-163: When shifting to active attacks, the gains are even more substantial.
elements.sent-2411-164: For instance, when the attack is active for 50 rounds, we achieve gains of 13, 22, and 5 percentage points (p.p.) in Income-L, Income-A, and Medical, respectively, over Grad-w-O, and even larger gains over the more realistic Grad.
elements.sent-2411-165: Furthermore, the attack accuracy reaches the performance expected from an adversary who knows the optimal local model.
elements.sent-2411-166: Interestingly, while our attacks consistently improve as the adversary's capacity increases (moving from a passive attacker to an active one and increasing the number of rounds of the active attack), this is not the case for gradient-based methods.

elements.sent-2411-167: We simulate varying levels of heterogeneity in the Income-L dataset and illustrate how the attack performance evolves in Figure~\ref{fig:impact (left).}
elements.sent-2411-168: First, we observe that as the data is more heterogeneously split, the accuracy of AIA improves for all approaches.
elements.sent-2411-169: Indeed, the data splitting strategy (Appendix~\ref{app:datasplit_incomel ) leads to a greater degree of correlation between the clients' sensitive attributes and the target variable at higher levels of heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-170: This intrinsic correlation facilitates the operation of all AIAs.
elements.sent-2411-171: We observe in these experiments, as in previous ones, that active Grad does not necessarily offer advantages over passive Grad.
elements.sent-2411-172: In the more homogeneous case (which is less realistic in an FL setting), our passive attack performs slightly worse than Grad, but its accuracy increases more rapidly with heterogeneity, resulting in an AIA accuracy advantage of over 20 p.p.
elements.sent-2411-173: Our active attack over 50 additional rounds consistently outperforms Grad by at least 30 p.p.
elements.sent-2411-174: Figure~\ref{fig:impact (center) shows that the performance of our attacks slightly decrease as the batch size increases.}
elements.sent-2411-175: A possible explanation for this is that a larger batch size results in fewer local updates per epoch, leading the client to return a less overfitted model.
elements.sent-2411-176: Despite this, our approach consistently outperforms Grad in all cases.
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Impact of the number of local epochs.
elements.sent-2411-177: Figure~\ref{fig:impact (right) shows that under passive attacks, our approach is not sensitive to the number of local epochs, whereas Grad's performance deteriorates to the level of random guessing as the number of local epochs increases to 5.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Interestingly, active Grad significantly improves upon passive Grad as the number of local epochs increases.
elements.sent-2411-179: While our active approach progressively performs slightly worse, it still maintains an advantage of over 10 p.p.~compared to Grad.
elements.paragraph-2411-010: Impact of the local dataset size.
elements.sent-2411-180: The experiment setting is the same as in Table~\ref{tab:attacks_32 with 50 active rounds.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Moreover, for clients with more than 20000 data points, Grad provides an attack performance close to random guessing.
elements.sent-2411-182: To mitigate privacy leakage, we apply a federated version of DP-SGD~\cite{DP , which provides sample-level differential privacy guarantees~\cite{Dwork, slDP .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Our experiments show that even with this defense mechanism in place, our approach still outperforms the baselines in most of the scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-185: In our work, we have demonstrated the effectiveness of using model-based AIA for federated regression tasks, when the attacker can approximate the optimal local model.
elements.sent-2411-186: For an honest-but-curious adversary, we proposed a computationally efficient approach to reconstruct the optimal linear local model in least squares regression.
elements.sent-2411-187: In contrast, for neural network scenarios, our passive approach involves directly utilizing the last returned model (Sec.~\ref{subsec:baseline ).}
elements.sent-2411-188: We believe more sophisticated reconstruction techniques may exist, and we plan to investigate this aspect in future work.
elements.sent-2411-189: The reader may wonder if the superiority of model-based attacks over gradient-based ones also holds for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-190: Some preliminary experiments we conducted suggest that the relationship is inverted.
elements.sent-2411-191: We advance a partial explanation for this observation.
elements.sent-2411-192: For a linear model with binary cross-entropy loss, it can be shown that the model-based AIA \eqref{eq:aia_model_based on a binary sensitive attribute is equivalent to a simple label-based attack, where the attribute is uniquely reconstructed based on the label.}
elements.sent-2411-193: This approach leads to poor performance because the attack relies only on general population characteristics and ignores individual specificities.
elements.sent-2411-194: This observation also holds experimentally for neural networks trained on the Income-L dataset, largely due to the inherent unfairness of the learned models.
elements.sent-2411-195: For example, for the same set of public features, being a man consistently results in a higher probability of being classified as wealthy compared to being a woman.
elements.sent-2411-196: As a consequence, the AIA infers gender solely based on the high or low income label.
elements.sent-2411-197: These preliminary remarks may prompt new interesting perspectives on the relationship between fairness and privacy (see for example the seminal paper~\cite{chang2021privacy ).}
elements.sent-2411-198: Last but not least, to mitigate privacy leakage, beyond differential privacy mechanisms, there are empirical defenses such as Mixup~\cite{mixup , which trains a model on composite data samples obtained through linear combinations of sample pairs, and TAPPFL~\cite{aaai_defense , which learns low-dimensional representations that minimize information about private attributes.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: However, the effectiveness of these defenses has not yet been demonstrated on regression tasks, and we leave this for future work.

elements.subsection-2411-015: Data splitting strategy for Income-L dataset
elements.sent-2411-306: For Income-L dataset, we apply a splitting strategy to control statistical heterogeneity among the 10 clients, which is detailed in Alg.~\ref{algo:Income-L .}
elements.sent-2411-307: To achieve this, we first partition the initial dataset into two clusters, $\mathcal{D _{h}$ and $\mathcal{D _{l}$, which exhibit strong opposing correlations between the sensitive attribute and the target income.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: More precisely, $\mathcal{D _h$ contains samples of rich men and poor women and $\mathcal{D _{l}$ contains samples of poor men and rich women (lines~2-3).}}
elements.sent-2411-309: We then randomly select $ \min(|\mathcal{D _{h |,|\mathcal{D _{l |)$ samples from each cluster to have balanced size, denoted by $\mathcal{D '_h$ and $\mathcal{D '_h$ (lines~5-6).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Initially, $\mathcal{D '_h$ and $\mathcal{D '_l$ have clearly different distributions.}}
elements.sent-2411-311: By randomly swapping a fraction of $0.5-h$ of samples between the two clusters (lines~7-8), the distributions of $\mathcal{D '_h$ and $\mathcal{D '_l$ become more similar as $h$ decreases.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: Lastly, each cluster is divided equally into datasets for five clients (lines~11-12).
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Splitting strategy for Income-L dataset with heterogeneity level $h \in [0, 0.5]$}\label{algo:Income-L} Input: the initial Income-L dataset $\mathcal{D}=\{(\mathbf{x}^p(i),s(i), y(i)), i=1,..., |\mathcal{D}|\}$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE $\mathrm{med} \leftarrow$ median value in $\{y(i), i=1,..., |\mathcal{D}|\}$ \STATE Let $\mathcal{D}_{h}= \{((\mathbf{x}^p(i),s(i), y(i)) \in \mathcal{D}\,\,| ( s(i) = \mathrm{man} \land y(i) > \mathrm{med} )\lor ( s(i) = \mathrm{woman} \land y(i) <= \mathrm{med}) \}$ \STATE Let $\mathcal{D}_{l} = \mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_{h}$ \STATE $k \leftarrow \min(|\mathcal{D}_{h}|,|\mathcal{D}_{l}|)$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{h}' \leftarrow$ sample randomly $k$ points from $\mathcal{D}_{h}$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{l}' \leftarrow$ sample randomly $k$ points from $\mathcal{D}_{l}$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{hs}' \leftarrow$ sample randomly $(0.5-h)k$ points from $\mathcal{D}_{h}'$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{ls}' \leftarrow$ sample randomly $(0.5-h)k$ points from $\mathcal{D}_{l}'$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{h}'\leftarrow (\mathcal{D}_{h}' \setminus \mathcal{D}_{hs}') \cup \mathcal{D}_{ls}'$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}_{l}'\leftarrow$ $(\mathcal{D}_{l}' \setminus \mathcal{D}_{ls}') \cup \mathcal{D}_{hs}'$ \STATE Split $\mathcal{D}_{l}'$ equally among the first 5 clients \STATE Split $\mathcal{D}_{h}'$ equally among the last 5 clients \end{algorithmic}
